articles.title: 'It's more than a hope, it's a guarantee': The Vera C. Rubin Observatory's 10-year movie of the universe is about to 'blow our minds,' chief scientist Tony Tyson says
articles.summary: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory has officially begun its Legacy Survey of Space and Time, a 10-year mission to map the entire southern sky many times over. Chief Scientist Tony Tyson tells us why the survey could be remembered 100 years from now, if bright corporate satellites d...
articles.content:
On a dark mountaintop in Chile, the world's largest digital camera has begun filming its masterpiece.

This Tuesday (June 30), scientists with the Vera C. Rubin Observatory announced that the facility's ambitious Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST) officially began. Every night for the next 10 years, the observatory's car-size LSST Camera will capture a 3,200-megapixel image of the southern sky — then another, then another, slowly filling in a mosaic of the universe 30 seconds at a time.

"In a sense, we're taking a digital color motion picture of the universe," Tony Tyson, a professor of cosmology at the University of California Davis and LSST's chief scientist and former founding director of the Rubin Observatory, told Live Science.

Strafing across the sky in stop-motion, the survey is expected to spot between 7 million and 8 million changes among the stars each night — from flashing supernovas and streaking comets to colliding galaxies and dim, tumbling asteroids. Within minutes of each exposure, alerts to any peculiar changes will become publicly available for astronomers and space enthusiasts around the world to study.

"Rubin is an automated facility, so scientists don't come here to use it," Tyson said. "But tens of trillions of observations is enough data for everybody in the world."

Tyson is hoping the firehose of data will unmask theinvisible 95% of the universe that is composed of dark matter and dark energy.

While there are still some technical bugs to work out — and the looming threat of ultra-bright corporate satellites to contend with — Tyson and his colleagues are ready to roll out the survey, "gradually increasing our sky area and image quality" over the next few months, he added.

Get the world’s most fascinating discoveries delivered straight to your inbox.

Live Science spoke with Tyson about the LSST and what it may find in the coming years.

Brandon Specktor: You're about to spend 10 years observing the southern sky with the largest digital camera ever. What will a typical night of the survey look like?

Tony Tyson: In a sense, we're making a digital color motion picture of the universe. We'll take thousands of 30-second exposures every night. Within two minutes of the shutter closing on an exposure, we will process all the data, [compare] it from the archival sky of that piece of the sky, and — if something explodes, or pops off, or moves in the sky in a way we don't understand — issue an alert. The alerts go to the world.

I made the decision early on to make the data available to everyone. The alerts will also go to eight data brokers, which specialize in things like cosmology, supernovae, etc., and the public can sign up to the feed from their favorite brokers.

BS: Which feed will you be watching most closely?

TT: My most interesting data broker is one which will come up with a classification of "unknown." I'm more interested in the unknown, unclassifiable things that go on in the universe. But there's a sort of a live stream of catalog information that's going to be available to people — and we're excited for writing up a lot of new discoveries.

My hope at this time is that we will discover something unexpected that will revolutionize astronomy. I think it's more than a hope, I think it's a guarantee.

BS: What kind of revolution would that be? Is there one big question you hope Rubin will answer?

TT: I'm a cosmologist, so my hope is that we'll get closer to understanding the physics of dark energy and dark matter.

But to be frank, I think we'll be remembered 100 years from now for something else in addition. And discovering something totally new in the time domain, something that blows our minds, that we did not expect — some kind of new object that's out there. There's examples of this in radio astronomy with the FRBs [fast radio bursts], for example. And I think that that's going to be how we will be remembered.

BS: The Rubin Observatory observes in optical light. Are there any strange optical phenomena you have your eye on?

TT: There's already a pretty strong hint, actually, that there is a population of very faint bursting objects that just pop off. The ones we know about are typically pretty bright — supernovae, which last for a long time, and gamma-ray bursts, which are bright but they don't last very long. But there's a big question mark in this unique area that we're going to explore, of faint things that are very short-lived. And there's evidence now from a team in Japan that there is a population of very faint things that explode just once. They don't repeat. And so I'm keeping my eye on that.

BS: What are the Rubin Observatory's main science goals?

TT: There's a lot of areas of science that we can address — and that was really the main selling point for us with the agencies. For one, we'll be looking at cosmology and the history of the expansion of the universe. Just by itself, we will have enough data to measure key parameters in cosmology to eliminate models of dark matter and dark energy, which is exciting.

Another area is looking at new kinds of stars in our galaxy, so we can look at the history of our galaxy and the history of star formation in our galaxy. At even lower redshift, an interesting area is looking at both comets and potentially Earth-threatening asteroids. Every night we detect about a thousand new asteroids.

And we will be able to uniquely look for those, if we can get rid of the interfering low-Earth-orbiting satellites, which really make that impossible. And so I've been unfortunately sidetracked into worrying a lot about that in the recent year.

BS: Are you talking about companies like Reflect Orbital, which essentially want to put giant mirrors in low Earth orbit? We've written about how satellites like that could totally compromise the LSST.

Yes. There's a proposed class of extremely ultra-bright satellites that are going to be launched that are incompatible with the LSST science, totally incompatible. The skies will no longer be dark for anybody, anywhere.

Reflect Orbital is one example. The other example is these orbiting AI [artificial intelligence] computational centers, which will be exceedingly bright. We've met with all these companies. They say that they feel our pain, but their board of directors or their investors say that they're going to go forward.

I've been working with SpaceX, though. They're really trying very hard to eliminate some of these effects, but nothing is perfect. It's going to be tough.

BS: So if these companies are moving forward, what are you going to do?

What I'm trying to do is work very closely with Congress and the American Astronomical Society and other bodies — the United Nations, the FCC [Federal Communications Commission] — to see what we can do.

I'm inherently an optimist. I think the Reflect Orbital is a failed business model, but they're going to try doing it anyway. So they'll put a lot of junk up there for a while.

Related stories

  • First Vera Rubin Observatory image reveals hidden structure as long as the Milky Way trailing behind a nearby galaxy — Space photo of the week
  • Vera C. Rubin Observatory discovers enormous, record-breaking asteroid in first 7 nights of observations
  • 'Revolutionary': Vera C. Rubin Observatory found 800,000 objects of interest in a single night

BS: I truly wish you the best with that. But since you're an optimist, let's end on something positive. You've championed this observatory for more than 20 years — first as its founding director and now its chief scientist. How does it feel now that the LSST is finally operational?

It's quite gratifying, after all this time, to have something that actually works. It's a hugely complicated system, and nothing so complicated as that works perfectly all the time.

I was the original founding director, I am now the chief scientist, and it is my day job to worry about what's going wrong with this or that. And there's a laundry list of things that we're worried about. But it's working, and it's working quite well. And so that's quite gratifying.

Editor's Note: This interview has been condensed and edited for clarity

Brandon is the space / physics editor at Live Science. With more than 20 years of editorial experience, his writing has appeared in The Washington Post, Reader's Digest, CBS.com, the Richard Dawkins Foundation website and other outlets. He holds a bachelor's degree in creative writing from the University of Arizona, with minors in journalism and media arts. His interests include black holes, asteroids and comets, and the search for extraterrestrial life.

articles.title: America at 500: Where will we be in space in 2276?
articles.summary: When the U.S. was born, humanity was still seven years away from balloon-borne flight. Where might we be another 250 years from now, should the nation be fortunate enough to survive that long?
articles.content:
The United States has taken some significant steps into the final frontier during its first 250 years.

The nation has put people on the moon, helped build and operate a long-running space station in low Earth orbit (LEO) and sent fleets of robotic explorers to many corners of the solar system — and even beyond it, into interstellar space.

All of this work has been done relatively recently, as the space age didn't dawn until 1957; when the U.S. was born on July 4, 1776, humanity was still seven years away from even balloon-borne flight. Where might we be another 250 years from now, on the nation's 500th birthday, should it be fortunate enough to live that long? Trying to see that far into the future is so difficult as to be a fool's errand, but it's fun. So let's have a brief and far from exhaustive crack.

A vibrant in-space economy

The United States and other space powers have already established an off-Earth economy — one based on the activities of communications satellites. Companies like Vantor and Planet sell imagery to a variety of customers, for example, while others like SpaceX (via its subsidiary Starlink) and Viasat provide internet service from above.

That nascent industry will doubtless expand greatly over the next 250 years, and we're already seeing some of the directions it may go. For instance, space tourism has gotten off the ground; wealthy people can book trips to suborbital space, and the super-rich can fly all the way to Earth orbit, as the experience of NASA chief Jared Isaacman shows. (Isaacman, a tech billionaire, has funded and commanded two missions around our planet using SpaceX hardware.)

We've also seen the dawn of in-space manufacturing, with companies such as Made In Space making stuff off Earth and bringing it down for analysis (and eventually, if all goes to plan, sale). This is a field that could really explode over the coming years and decades, according to Dava Newman, director of the Human Systems Lab at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, who served as deputy administrator of NASA from 2015 to 2017.

"If you give me a nice big time horizon to look at, I've actually always thought it would be a pharmaceutical breakthrough — manufacturing, more medical-related," Newman told Space.com.

That's because the microgravity environment is great for growing flawless crystals, potentially enabling a newly efficient and effective production line for a wide range of pharmaceuticals and other high-value goods. The California company Varda Space recently demonstrated this potential, successfully crystallizing a stable form of the HIV drug ritonavir in one of its orbital "minifactories" and bringing the drug safely down to Earth.

Asteroid mining, too

There are other potential drivers of a booming off-Earth economy as well. Futurist, astrophysicist and sci-fi author David Brin pointed to asteroid mining, which several American companies, including AstroForge and TransAstra, are investigating seriously already.

"That's where the riches are," Brin told Space.com.

Those riches come in several forms. For starters, many asteroids are thought to harbor considerable amounts of water, which humanity could leverage for life support and split into oxygen and hydrogen, key components of rocket fuel. Space-rock mining could therefore enable the operation of off-Earth propellant depots, which would allow voyaging spacecraft to top off their tanks on the go and explore the solar system more deeply and ambitiously.

Then there are the metals — industrial-grade stuff like iron and nickel, which could feed the off-Earth manufacturing industry, and precious species such as platinum. So there are huge economic opportunities for us in the asteroid belt, according to Brin.

“The question is, will we be able to leverage asteroidal resources to get a takeoff industry out there?" he said. "And will we remain friends with the robots out there that are doing all of the work?”

That latter question is a serious one, for robotics and artificial intelligence will advance a great deal over the next 250 years. Indeed, humanity will likely merge with robots in meaningful and ethically confounding ways, according to Brin.

"There will be fuzzy boundaries between us and the robots," he said. Our species, Brin added, may eventually "range from residual totally organic types through cyborgs and multilinked clusters all the way to robots who either think of themselves as human citizens, or at least know that it's in their best interest to fool us into thinking they think that."

Living off Earth

There's no guarantee that the U.S. will be able to exploit the plentiful resources of the asteroid belt by 2276. For instance, Brin stressed that the nation, and the world at large, are in danger of falling into a sort of "lobotomized feudalism," which could derail most of our spaceflight hopes and dreams.

If we can avoid that trap, however, Brin thinks that asteroid mining could fuel our expansion into the solar system over the coming decades.

"I have no doubt that, if we restore a rational, scientific civilization, there will be city lights on the moon," Brin said. "I am hoping there will be city lights on the asteroids."

Newman has a different outlook. For starters, she's opposed to the idea of transplanting human civilization on a large scale to pristine worlds.

"I'm not a fan, of course, of colonization," she said. "We should never do that. History should teach us something."

She also doesn't see a business case that would drive companies to spend large amounts of money on moon and Mars activities, which could be prerequisites for the establishment of large human settlements there. And she's skeptical that many people would want to pack up their lives and move to Mars or the moon permanently.

"There's a reason Antarctica is not populated," Newman said. "I love it, I can't get enough of it, but that's pretty crazy. Most people don't want to be in a very isolated, confined environment."

Mars settlement, she added, "doesn't make any sense. It's not Option B. We have to take care of this planet."

Newman still thinks that we should (and will) explore the moon and Mars in the not-too-distant future. But she envisions a much smaller-scale effort that's driven by ambitious science goals — a committed search for signs of life on Mars, for example. This effort will likely start with small outposts on the moon, which will serve as stepping stones for similar activities on the Red Planet.

This vision is not exactly far-fetched; it's the approach that NASA is currently taking with its Artemis program. The agency plans to build an astronaut outpost near the lunar south pole over the next decade or so, then use the information gained to send astronauts to Mars in the late 2030s or 2040s.

A moon or Mars base would be a huge breakthrough, of course, but it wouldn't be our species' first toehold in the final frontier. We've had one since November 2000, when the International Space Station — a partnership involving the space agencies of the U.S., Russia, Europe, Canada and Japan — began hosting astronauts on a continuous basis. The orbiting lab has been occupied by rotating crews ever since.

Are we alone?

The U.S., and the world at large, will doubtless make great strides in space science over the coming years as well. Indeed, Newman thinks we will answer perhaps the biggest question of all — and doing so won't take anywhere close to 250 years.

"I think definitely we will have found [alien] life," she said. "I think we might find the evidence of life — and it's probably going to be past life — in the coming decade."

Her optimism is based on a number of data points. One is the example of our own planet: Life sprang up here about four billion years ago, not long after Earth had cooled enough to support oceans on its surface. That suggests it doesn't take a miracle for a world to go from habitable to inhabited.

And our solar system hosts multiple worlds that could be habitable. Multiple moons in the outer solar system — Saturn's Enceladus and Jupiter's Europa, for example — host big liquid-water oceans beneath their icy shells. Titan, Saturn's biggest satellite, hosts hydrocarbon lakes and streams on its surface and likely has a buried water ocean as well, raising the possibility that it could support two entirely different types of life.

Then there's Mars. Scientists know that the Red Planet had lots of surface water in the distant past, around four billion years ago. They see evidence of lakes and streams in many Martian locales, some of which have been explored by rovers like Curiosity and Perseverance. And both of those NASA robots have discovered complex organics, the carbon-containing building blocks of life as we know it. Such molecules are not convincing evidence of life, but they're suggestive and intriguing. And humanity aims to follow up on such finds — possibly by astronauts working out of a research base. Mars is therefore the place where we'll likely make the big discovery, Newman said.

Brin was similarly optimistic, predicting that we'll find evidence of alien life in the next 20 years "if we reclaim our potential as an exploratory culture." Mars could be the place it's found, but he thinks the chances are perhaps even greater on Titan and "ice roof" worlds like Europa and Enceladus.

"I would bet 3 to 1 odds we'll find life under ice roofs," Brin said.

If multiple icy moons have life, and we can confirm that each one represents an independent origin, that would tell us something very profound: That life is exceedingly common across the universe.

"It would mean that every star you can see, except perhaps the blue supergiants, has life," Brin said.

The search for intelligent life

The first alien lifeforms we find will probably be microbes, because most life across the universe is likely microbial. That's an inference we can take from Earth, the only inhabited world we know of: Life here remained single-celled for about three billion years, suggesting it's tough to make the leap to more complex organisms.

Becoming a technological civilization is another big leap, one that humanity made just a few centuries ago. But given the vastness of the universe, in both time and space, life has probably made this leap in other places as well, and Brin is bullish on our chances of crossing paths with such aliens — provided we make the right choices in the near future.

“If we restore a dynamic and scientific civilization, we might get some answers to that within 20 to 30 years," he said.

Those answers might come close to home, in the form of long-hidden "lurker" probes sent to our solar system to monitor us on the sly. Or we might pull a world-changing SETI (search for extraterrestrial intelligence) signal from the sky, one that could not have come from a natural, astrophysical source.

Given how young humans are technologically, however — we started launching things to space less than 70 years ago, after all — we probably won't get the drop on advanced aliens, if they're out there.

"Is it likely that intelligent life will actually find us before we find them? I think that's a pretty good probability," said Newman, who sits on the SETI Institute's board of directors.

And we have a chance to mature greatly as a spacefaring civilization over the next 250 years, Brin said. If everything goes well — we don't fall back into feudalism, for example, and we fully exploit the resources of the asteroid belt — the U.S., and humanity overall, we will likely be able to explore other star systems in a meaningful way. He cited laser sailing as a promising propulsion method, one that could send robots — and perhaps cybernetic versions of ourselves — very far afield on reasonable timescales.

"If we make a dynamic, enlightenment civilization, then it's 100% that we'll send somebody out there," Brin said.

Michael Wall is the Spaceflight and Tech Editor for Space.com and joined the team in 2010. He primarily covers human and robotic spaceflight, military space, and exoplanets, but has been known to dabble in the space art beat. His book about the search for alien life, "Out There," was published on Nov. 13, 2018. Before becoming a science writer, Michael worked as a herpetologist and wildlife biologist. He has a Ph.D. in evolutionary biology from the University of Sydney, Australia, a bachelor's degree from the University of Arizona, and a graduate certificate in science writing from the University of California, Santa Cruz. articles.title: Evaluation of damage intensity in concrete-filled steel tubular (CFST) columns using experimental dynamic data
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 03 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-43483-y Evaluation of damage intensity in concrete-filled steel tubular (CFST) columns using experimental dynamic data
articles.content:
Abstract

Given the importance of damage identification in structures, this research presents a laboratory modal data-based method. After conducting experiments on intact and damaged specimens (Four types of artificial damages; two healthy specimens and eight damaged specimens), primary modal parameters such as mode shapes, natural frequencies, and damping ratios were extracted. Tools such as the Model Assurance Control(MAC) and frequency domain analysis were then employed to detect and confirm the presence of damage. Furthermore, this study utilized two criteria—the Modal Phase Collinearity (MPC) and Mode Shape Displacement Depth (MSDD)—to evaluate the intensity of damage in each specimen and its variations across mode shapes. The results successfully demonstrate the detection of artificial damages in CFST columns using the proposed method. According to the findings, the DHBP specimen exhibited a lower MSDD index (Reflecting the normalized progression of mode shapes) compared to other specimens, attributed to higher damage severity and the absence of shear connectors along the column. The results indicate that shear connectors in CFST columns can effectively hinder damage propagation during early stages of minor damage, thereby preserving the structural integrity of the column. In DDBP, the one exhibiting the largest amplitude is identified. The presence of shear connectors between the concrete and the steel tube partially eliminates the issue of weak separation and lack of adhesion. However, the lowest value is still assigned to the specimen that has an empty space within the steel tube, because the shear connectors cannot provide the desired stiffness supplied by the concrete, thus causing the value of the ratio L2/L1 to decrease.

Introduction

Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) is vital in civil engineering for enhancing safety, preventing failures, reducing maintenance costs, and extending structural lifespan. Early damage detection helps avoid crises, support retrofitting, and identify design or construction flaws, making the development of advanced methods for detecting subtle, early-stage damage a key research focus.

Structural damages in historical and cultural heritage buildings1, concrete, steel, and composite structures pose significant challenges and risks to the structural safety and functional performance of these critical engineering elements. Being aware of various structural damages for ensuring structural durability and integrity the identification and comprehension of various types of structural damage are critical for ensuring the durability and integrity of structures. This research aims to investigate the types of damage that may occur in CFST columns, including, corrosion leading to partial loss of the steel Sects2,3,4,5,6,7, fatigue and steel shell distortion8, debonding of the concrete core from the steel tube due to earthquakes and other seismic loads9. By gaining a thorough understanding of structural damages and failures, engineers and researchers can enhance the resilience and safety of structures10. The application of signal-based methods (WVD and HT) has been evaluated for the rapid damage assessment of reinforced concrete bridge piers following an earthquake. These techniques estimate the instantaneous frequency by analyzing recorded accelerations from shaking table tests conducted on damaged columns. The Hilbert Transform (HT) is enhanced via modal filtering. The findings demonstrate that both methods are effective in large-amplitude response; specifically, the WVD exhibits superior resolution, while the HT is more computationally efficient, with its robustness significantly improved through noise suppression11,12. However, most existing vibration- or modal-based indices (e.g., MAC, COMAC, MPC) are limited in their ability to quantify the severity of damage or to differentiate subtle changes in mode shape curvature.

Concrete-Filled Steel Tube (CFST) columns are widely used in high-rise buildings due to their good performance under seismic loads and reduced cross-sectional area. To address connection limitations, proposals have included introducing external stiffeners13, trapezoidal and horizontal rebar mats14,15, and various plates for CFST beam-column connections to provide alternative load transfer paths. The seismic parameters and behavior of connections with internal and external stiffeners have also been investigated16,17,18,19.

CFST columns with axial loading and T-shaped cross-sections have been studied by several researchers. These investigations focused on two types of T-shaped columns; ordinary CFST columns with T-shaped cross-sections, Double Concrete-Filled Steel Tube T-shaped Columns (DCFSTTC). The results show that the both column types exhibit similar failure modes, characterized by outward buckling of the steel tubes across all column faces20,21,22.

A Number of researchers proposed a damage-sensitive index for locating damage in structures. One technique for crack detection in plates is based on 2D modal curvature24. They improved the 2D modal curvature technique using real and complex wavelet transforms. Another study used optimization techniques, Moth Flame Optimization(MFO) with modal assurance criterion and natural frequencies25. A method for beamlike structures using modal curvature estimation was efficient in locating damage. Khan Ahmadi et al.26 used vibration mode shapes to locate damage in axially loaded columns. Yulong et al.27 applied wavelet and curvelet transforms for denoising images in thin plate damage detection; curvelet performed better than wavelet due to energy concentration. A damage detection approach for bridge deck steel beams used distribution and matching of Born-Jordan coefficients, especially correlation coefficients26. Other methods model enrichment for elastodynamic problems in frequency domain29, a device for remote damage and crack monitoring30, and wavelet-based feature extraction for thin steel plates31,32.

Modal dynamic properties—including shifts in natural frequencies, mode shapes, and modal curvature—are established as sensitive indicators for detecting structural damage, particularly interfacial debonding between the concrete core and the steel tube. Work published in 2024 highlighted that modal curvature-based irregularity detection indices, which use changes in frequency and mode shapes, can effectively identify debonding zones in CFST columns, proving that damage modifies both frequency values and the spatial pattern of modal irregularities33. Furthermore, structural health monitoring research stresses the value of modal testing and vibration-based indices that merge dynamic and modal data to pinpoint stiffness reductions, which is the core process for damage detection in composite columns34. Moving beyond purely modal analysis, data-driven methods that process dynamic measurements from impact or vibration tests are being developed to automate and increase the accuracy of defect identification, including new machine learning models for real-time damage assessment based on vibration responses35. In 2025, a study proposed a dual-branch Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework that fuses multi-modal features specifically to detect subsurface interface defects in CFST members, showing better sensitivity to hidden damage than traditional modal analysis might achieve36. Finally, ongoing work in 2026 is focusing on using impact-acoustic data combined with unsupervised learning for intelligent detection of debonding and voids, enabling internal damage detection without extensive manual labeling, which aligns well with advanced imaging techniques37.

These advancements reflect a broader trend toward synthesizing dynamic data (impact/vibration time histories) with modal characteristics (frequency shifts, mode shape changes), often enhanced by modern data analytics and machine learning, to achieve more reliable and automated damage identification in CFST columns under various operating conditions. To improve upon current detection methods, this study introduces a new parameter, the Mode Shape Depth Difference (MSDD), which captures both amplitude and phase variations of normalized mode shapes, offering enhanced sensitivity to the degree of damage.

Despite the significant progress in vibration-based damage detection, existing indices such as MAC, COMAC, and MPC primarily focus on the presence or location of damage rather than its severity. Moreover, limited attention has been given to how the geometric characteristics of CFST columns influence modal irregularities under damaged conditions. To overcome these challenges, the present study proposes a new modal-based index, referred to as the Mode Shape Depth Difference (MSDD), which quantifies the variation of normalized mode shapes between intact and damaged states, thereby providing a more consistent measure of damage severity. This approach aims to enhance conventional modal damage characterization methods and improve the reliability of structural health monitoring in CFST systems.

Experimental Testing Program

Specimens and Materials

This study investigates 10 CFST columns with specifications detailed in Table 1; Fig. 1. The specimens are categorized into two groups:

  • Five specimens with shear connectors: Reinforced with 8 mm diameter rebar arranged in orthogonal directions along the column length(Connected).

  • Five specimens without shear connectors(Plain).

In this study, to artificial damage simulation, four common CFST column failure modes were artificially simulated to assess structural vulnerability:

  • a.

    DDB (Damage/DeBonding-concrete core detachment): Simulated interfacial separation between the concrete core and steel tube.

  • b.

    DGB (Damage/Global/Buckling-steel tube loss): Partial removal of steel tube thickness to mimic corrosion effects.

  • c.

    DLB (Damage/Local/Buckling-steel shell distortion): Induced geometric imperfections in the outer steel shell.

  • d.

    DHB (Damage/Hollow/Bulk-Concrete Core Voids): Artificial voids created in the concrete core to simulate poor compaction. In addition, a pristine control specimen was fabricated and tested as a baseline reference(UnDamage Specimen-UDS).

The main materials used in the specimens are steel and concrete, and polystyrene is used to simulate the separation of the steel shell from the concrete core and the void space in the concrete(DHBP, C Specimen). The mechanical properties of the materials are presented in Table 2.

Experimental setup

To conduct the test, the structure must undergo vibration. Various tools can be used for vibrational excitation; in this study, an impact hammer was employed. Since the presence of supports in the structure requires significantly larger excitation forces which may lead to computational errors or minor structural damage, most research utilizes free-free boundary conditions for modal testing. In this experiment, the column was placed on an elastic sponge surface, and the specimens were excited by the impact hammer. The frequency response function of each excitation was recorded by a data logger, evaluated on a computer, and finally, modal data were extracted. Figure 2 illustrates the experimental setup.

Results and discussion

Experimental observations indicate that specimens experience frequency reduction and minor alterations in mode shape structure following damage exposure. Frequency variations in shear connector-equipped specimens demonstrate lower magnitude due to their enhanced shear stiffness and better preservation of structural integrity post-damage.

General observations

Modal parameters including mode shapes and frequencies were extracted as primary experimental data. Analysis of mode shape alterations and frequency shifts enables damage detection in structural systems, with additional capability to localize damage and estimate its severity. Figure 3 comparatively present fundamental mode shapes for specimens with/without shear connectors.

According to Fig. 3, specimen DHBP exhibits the shortest slope length (defined as the distance between the arc apex and the start/end points) due to its higher damage level compared to other specimens. This indicates that structural damage reduces stiffness, leading to the formation of the first deformation mode with less displacement and requiring 67% less energy for activation.

In contrast, specimen DLBC demonstrates the longest slope length, attributed to two key factors; lower damage level, which preserves structural stiffness and shear connectors, which enhance steel-concrete cohesion and restore stiffness in composite columns. The normalized deformation of DLBC is 67% greater than DHBP, highlighting the critical role of shear connectors in maintaining structural integrity under reduced damage conditions. In specimen DHBP, the partial removal of concrete from the column section results in simultaneous reductions in both mass and stiffness. Crucially, the mass decreases more significantly than stiffness, leading to an increase in the mass-to-stiffness ratio. This altered ratio amplifies the slope of the mode shape curve in both halves of the structure, causing the curve to reach its peak displacement over a shorter span compared to undamaged configurations.

In the second mode of the specimens with shear connector, the distance between the vertices of the arches in the DHBC has the highest value, while in the specimens without shear connector, the DGBP and DLBP specimens have the highest value. In the third mode, the DLBC has the highest distance, but in the case without shear connector, the DDBP has the highest displacement due to the severe reduction in stiffness and the absence of shear connector to maintain the integrity of concrete and steel and the disruption of their simultaneous performance, which shows the importance of the presence of shears in CFT columns in controlling the most common damage (detachment) in the aforementioned columns.

In all specimens except for the DHBP, there is no additional curvature, and the mode shape is almost uniform. The reason for this behavior is the presence of three materials (concrete, steel, and polystyrene) with different mechanical properties. When the specimen is stimulated, each exhibits distinct behaviors, and ultimately, according to the principle of superposition and the uniqueness of the overall behavior of the column, such a phenomenon arises, which in itself indicates the presence of damage in the structure. On the other hand, the intensity of damage in this specimen is greater than in the others because a large volume of concrete has been replaced by a much weaker and more porous material. Since reaching the first mode is easier in experimental research and real-world studies compared to other modes, and also because the occurrence of the first mode under various loadings (such as earthquakes) is more likely than that of other modes, examining other modes is less feasible for identifying damage and retrofitting, making it a time-consuming process that requires significant costs.

Therefore, this research specifically focuses on examining the first mode in both cases, with and without shear connectors. In present study, Frequency Response Function (FRF) diagrams have been extracted(Fig. 4), in which noticeable changes are observed at the resonance points, indicating the main frequencies of each specimen. The number of peaks in the FRF curves indicates the number of degrees of freedom of the system. The general shape of these curves indicates that this structure has an infinite number of degrees of freedom. Since the aim of the research is to investigate the structure in three main modes, the characteristics of the first three modes are presented.

Frequency and damping

Table 3 shows the frequencies of the specimens in the main modes. In specimens with higher damage levels, the frequency is higher in samples reinforced with shear connectors due to the effect of shear in unifying the behavior of the CFT column after damage compared to specimens without shear connectors, in other words, shear connectors help maintain the overall integrity of the columns after experiencing a major damage. However, in samples with minor damage, it has little effect and may even reduce the frequency because it increases the overall mass of the specimens. A decrease in frequency in the specimens indicates the presence of damage in them, which is clearly visible in the results. In DHBP and DHBC, the frequency has increased due to the placement of polystyrene instead of concrete at the bottom of the column and the reduction in the mass of the structure.

Table 3 presents the frequency and damping values, where the frequency is obtained from the FRF curve (Fig. 5) and the damping is obtained from the peak-peaking method (Eq. 1). In this method, the frequency difference between the two sides of the peak point of the FRF curve, i.e. point \(\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\alpha\) (\(\alpha\): the value of the peak point of the FRF curve), is divided by the fundamental frequency (peak point) and the result is multiplied by 100 (see Fig. 5).

Where;\(\zeta\)damping,\({\omega _a}\)arc starting point frequency,\({\omega _b}\)arc ending point frequency,\({\omega _r}\)peak point frequency. Another modal data for investigating damage in structures is Model Predictive Control (MPC), which is a type of advanced process control. Predictive controllers based on process dynamic models are mainly empirical linear models obtained by system identification. The most important advantage of the MPC is that it has the possibility of optimization38.

According to Table 3, the MPC value indicates the degree of complexity of the mode shape. The more realistic the mode shape, the closer it is to a MPC value. It should be noted that one of the reasons for the formation of a mixed mode shape is the non-uniform damping in the structure of the structure.

The mixed mode shape Unlike the true mode shape, it does not have a stable shape and moves in a wave-like manner throughout the structure with time. The MPC value in the first mode is close to one, which indicates the accuracy of the experiment and the quality of the data, on the other hand, in higher modes, the MPC value decreases, indicating that this mode is mixed. In addition, the structure of specimen is also affects the level of this parameter. According to observations, the MPC value is higher in specimens with shear connector than the specimens without shear connector, due to the integrated behavior of the steel tube and concrete core.

Changes in natural frequencies can be considered one of the common methods for damage identification in structural evaluation processes. When damage occurs in a structure, its stiffness decreases, leading to a measurable reduction in the system’s natural frequencies. The comparison of modal data (frequency and damping) is summarized in two separate tables for structures without shear connectors (Table 4) and with shear connectors (Table 5), as well as in Fig. 6. According to the results, the natural frequency in damaged specimens decrease compared to health specimens, which is attributed to structural damage and a reduction in the stiffness-to-mass ratio.

The highest frequency reduction (67.81%) compared to the healthy specimen (UDSP) occurred in the third mode of the DLBP, while the lowest reduction was observed in the second mode of the DLBP. Additionally, the DGBP exhibits the highest damping ratio, showing a reduction of approximately 79.70% compared to the healthy specimen (UDSP).

The lowest energy dissipation is observed in the DLBP specimen’s second mode, with a value of 0.209 (See Table 4). According to Table 5, in models equipped with connectors, the UDSC (Health specimen) in the third mode has the highest frequency of 3277.5 Hz, while the DLBC in the first mode exhibits the lowest frequency of 623.5 Hz. Furthermore, the DDBC shows the greatest reduction in damping ratio compared to the healthy specimen across all modes.

Since the frequency of each specimen depends on the stiffness-to-mass ratio (\(\omega =\sqrt {{\raise0.7ex\hbox{$k$} \!\mathord{\left/ {\vphantom {k m}}\right.\kern-0pt}\!\lower0.7ex\hbox{$m$}}}\)), in damaged specimens (except DDBP and DDBC), where mass decreases, one would expect frequency to increase. However, because the rate of mass reduction is unequal to the rate of stiffness loss, frequency may either decrease or increase. In DDBP and DDBC, mass does not decrease, but stiffness is reduced, leading to a frequency decrease compared to healthy specimens.

On the other hand, since stiffness is effectively rigidity per unit length, and given that the lengths of the specimens are equal, the equivalent cross-sectional rigidity of the specimens becomes the determining factor(Eq. 2).

Where, E is the modulus of elasticity, and I is the moment of inertia of the materials(steel and concrete).

A reduction in the cross-sectional area of a column due to damage causes a decrease in the moment of inertia parameter in the concrete or steel, and consequently in the entire section. This leads to a reduction in the equivalent rigidity of the column section, resulting in decreased stiffness, which ultimately manifests as a frequency reduction.

MAC criteria

This criterion examines the presence of damage in these structures by superimposing the modes of healthy and damaged specimens and multiplying them internally. The closer this value is to one, the greater the overlap of the modes under study. So that the number one indicates the complete correspondence of the mode shapes (vectors) and the number zero indicates that these vectors are perpendicular (Table 6). Operator MAC They investigate the modal behavior of the structure by point-to-point matching.

If the normal vector of the mode shapes of the undamaged and damaged specimens is out of alignment (zero angle), it means that damage has occurred in the structure and the value of these parameters is between one and zero.

In Fig. 7, the MAC plot of specimens are plotted relative to the healthy specimen. The elements of this graphical matrix represent the value of the parameter relative to the healthy specimen in the mode of interest. So that the sea (*)11 represents the result of adapting the first healthy mode to the first damaged mode. In general, the criteria for comparison are the elements on the main diameter of the matrix, because the other elements indicate the compatibility of each mode with modes other than itself, which is always a small number.

Effect depth of mode shape

The effect depth is a parameter that represents the ratio of the normalized positive to negative depth of the mode shape, and is obtained according to Fig. 8 and Eq. 3.

According to Fig. 8 and column L1+ L2 in Table 7, among the damaged specimens, DHBP has the smallest range and DDBP It has the largest amplitude, which has the highest and lowest damage severity in terms of modal data and difference with the healthy specimen, respectively.

On the other hand, the ratio (L2/L1)i/(L2/L1)b It is also like this. So it can be concluded that the greater the severity and depth of the damage, the smaller the deviation of the mode shape from the zero displacement point (see Fig. 9).

According to Figs. 10 and Fig. 11, DHBP has the largest opening at 80 cm of the column length, but DGBP has the largest distance between two arcs (approximately 1.75 units). In the third mode, the behavior is completely reversed, so that DGBP has the smallest distance between two arcs and the largest opening, which is due to the fact that this mode shape is mixed compared to the first two modes.

By comparing the mode shape of the specimen without shear connector and the specimen with shear connector in the corresponding modes, it is concluded that in the specimens with shear connectors, the distance between the apexes of the mode shape increases from both sides, which is due to the more integrated behavior of the column and the simultaneous performance of steel and concrete.

However, in the specimens without shear Connectors, after experiencing the load and after overcoming the adhesion between steel and concrete, the behaviors become separate and, depending on the type of damage, concrete or steel are decisive, and in general, this causes a decrease in the column strength (individual behavior), which leads to a decrease in the amplitude of the mode shape. In addition, clippers make the mode shape uniform and reduce possible jumps. In addition, these shear connectors prevent (or more accurately, reduce the rate of) damage, especially separation damage, in CFT columns.

According to Table 8, the distance between the end and start points in the DLBC is more than other specimens, which is due to the lack of a connectors at the site of damage and the distance between the connectors perpendicular to the damaged face on both sides of the damage. Unlike the DDBP Which has the largest amplitude, and if there is a shear connector, the weak separation and lack of adhesion between concrete and steel are partially eliminated by the presence of connectors in this area. But the lowest value is still assigned to the specimen with empty space in the steel tube because the shear connectors cannot provide the desired stiffness provided by the concrete, so this value (L2/L1) it decreases. In all modes of specimens with shear connector, the DLBC It has the most deformation, which is due to the absence of a shear connector in the area around the damage and the displacement of parallel grooves. In the model DGBC which has almost the same dynamic behavior and geometries as the model DLBP This behavior is not the case, and the reason for this can be attributed to the absence of a low-rigidity part (such as grooves created in the wall of the steel tube). In Table 9, the damping ratio parameters \(\frac{{{\zeta _i}}}{{{\zeta _{Health}}}}\), effect depth of mode shape L2/L1, frequency and MAC criteria of each specimen was compared to a healthy specimen and the results were presented.

The smaller the ratios in Table 9, the greater the difference in that parameter in the damaged sample compared to the healthy specimen. Parameter L2/L1 in the DHBP specimen, due to the greater severity of damage and the large reduction in stiffness and mass, it has the smallest value among the damaged specimens. DHBP has the largest value due to the lack of change in the mass of the structure and the smaller reduction in stiffness due to the separation of part of the steel wall from the concrete core, which indicates a greater similarity of the mode shape of this specimen compared to the UDSP. Similarly, in the MAC criteria, a similar result was obtained, indicating less overlap of mode shapes in the DHBP than in the UDSP.

Among the damaged specimens, the DGBP specimen achieved a 31.2% frequency reduction, which is due to the removal of part of the steel wall and the fact that steel has a higher modulus of elasticity than concrete and has a greater effect on frequency. According to Table 9, the damping ratio in all damaged specimens has decreased compared to the undamaged ones, which is due to the presence of damage and fewer cycles of energy dissipation by the damaged specimens. According to the Table 9, the reduction in damping in the DDBP and DLBP specimens was greater than in the other specimens, which is due to the separation of the plate from the concrete core and the failure of its integrated performancein the DDBP. In the DLBP specimen, two reasons cause this issue to occur, namely; the severity of the plate from the core at the crack site or the distortion of the plate due to the absence of a steel wall in a part of the structure and the loss of the thin steel strips remaining at the site of the damage. Figure 12 is arranged based on the increase in the frequency ratio of the damaged specimens to the healthy specimen in the first mode, and the changes in the two parameters L2/L1 and the damping ratio are compared.

According to Fig. 12, as the damping ratio increases, the effect depth ratio decreases. That is, in specimen where energy dissipation (damping) is greater, the effect depth is less, and vice versa. In Fig. 13, specimens that have had mass reduction due to damage are examined, and DDBP, which did not have mass reduction and only suffered damage due to loss of adhesion between concrete and steel, was excluded from the study.

The results of the study show that in specimens with a lower frequency reduction than the healthy specimen, the damping ratio decreases, so that specimens that have a greater frequency change than the healthy specimen have a lower damping coefficient. This result is valid for the case where the mass matrix changes in the frequency equation, but if the stiffness matrix changes due to factors such as cracking, the result is completely opposite. According to Fig. 14, which examines specimens with reduced mass, it can be concluded that the wavelet effect depth parameter (L2/L1) in proportion MAC it has a direct relationship.

So that these two parameters have almost the same curvature and their only difference is that the curve of changes MAC it has a larger transmission. On the other hand, it can be concluded that in specimens whose mode shape is more consistent with the healthy specimen, the length of the ratio parameter L2/L1 the greater the compliance, the less severe the damage, and the greater the effect depth of the wavelet(L2/L1) becomes less and the mode shape curve becomes more compact.

Figure 15 is arranged according to the amount of mass reduction. So that first the curve of DDBP specimen which has no mass reduction and the end of that DHBP Which has empty space in the concrete section amounting to 7.5% of the total mass of concrete.

According to Fig. 15, as the mass decreases, the L2/L1 ratio will also decrease, so that with a 67.5% reduction in the mass of the structure, the ratio L2/L1 it shows a decrease of 64.75%. On the other hand, it can be concluded that if the damages are classified based on the amount of mass reduction, the damping decreases with increasing frequency and vice versa.

Conclusion

Considering the possibility of damage and failure in the columns CFST due to the use of a concrete core and a steel tube, four types of possible damage in these structures have been investigated in this study. In this study, due to the use of free-free boundary conditions and an impact hammer, the results may not fully represent the actual behavior of the structure under operational conditions. Limitations such as dependency on the impact technique, environmental noise, and the effect of the foam support on vibration response can also reduce the accuracy of the modal data. Future studies can improve the precision and interpretation of structural dynamic behavior by employing more controlled excitations (such as an electromechanical shaker), increasing measurement points, performing numerical modeling for result validation, and investigating more realistic boundary conditions. The results of the evaluation are as follows:

  • In all damaged specimens, the mode shape and frequency have changed compared to the healthy specimen. This change was more pronounced in specimens with greater severity of damage. For example, in a healthy specimen(UDSP), the frequency is 808.4 Hz. and in the DGBP it has decreased by 12.94% to 703.79. A similar trend has been observed in other specimens as well.

  • The results show that in small damages, the greater the damage severity, the greater the damping of the structure, which indicates a decrease in the stiffness of the structure and a more ductile behavior of the structure.

  • Given that the MPC index it indicates the degree of complexity of the mode shape. The results show that this index will be a larger number in specimens with shears than in specimens without shear connectors due to the more integrated column due to the presence of connectors.

  • One of the parameter for identifying the presence of damage is the use of MAC criteria this parameter is a non-uniform number in damaged specimens and this number varies depending on the severity of the damage, so that in DDBP4 due to the debonding of the concrete cosre from the steel tube and in DHBP due to the removal of concrete in part of the inner core and its replacement with polystyrene, the difference in MAC values ​​is greater.

  • Among the damaged specimens, the mode shape of the DHBP has the lowest amplitude and DDBP has the largest amplitude, which has the highest and lowest damage severity in terms of modal data and difference with the healthy specimen, respectively. On the other hand, the ratio (L2/L1)i/(L2/L1)b is also like this. So it can be concluded that the greater the severity and depth of the damage, the smaller the deviation of the mode shape from the zero displacement point.

  • According to results, the DHBP specimen has the largest opening at 80 cm of the column length, but DGBP has the largest distance between two arcs (approximately 1.75 units). In the third mode, the behavior is completely reversed, so that DGBP has the smallest distance between two arcs and the largest opening, which is due to the fact that this mode shape is mixed compared to the first two modes.

Future studies should aim to improve structural dynamic analysis through more realistic boundary conditions, controlled excitations, and higher sensor density, supported by numerical modeling and advanced smart sensing for accurate and real-time monitoring, for example, constrained boundary conditions under axial load.

articles.title: Random noise attenuation using dual-module attention with multi-scale kernels
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 03 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-59772-5 Random noise attenuation using dual-module attention with multi-scale kernels
articles.content:
Abstract

Improving the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of seismic data is a fundamental problem in exploration geophysics, as random and coherent noise severely degrade seismic record quality and hinder subsequent imaging and interpretation. Conventional methods often suppress weak but geologically informative signals along with noise, limiting their effectiveness. This research presents a novel Attention module, the Multi-Kernel Channel-Spatial Attention (MKCSA), that effectively addresses random seismic data noise. The network enriched its feature space to extract the complex geological features, effectively attenuating random noise. Through the integration of multi-kernel features extraction into Channel and Spatial attention, which yields more efficient extraction of local and global geological features. This Attention mechanism amplifies noise suppression even further, thereby reducing the signal in the local similarity graph. Experimental results show that the MKCSA-Net outperforms Denoising Convolutional Neural Network (DnCNN) and the DnCNN with Convolutional Block Attention (DnCNN-CBAM) model, improving Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) and Frequency Distance (FD) by (\(4.82\%\), \(3.49\%\), and \(12.68\%\)) and (\(2.42\%\), \(1.77\%\), and \(7.46\%\)) respectively.

articles.title: Riemannian manifold dynamic attention fusion network for motor imagery EEG decoding
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 03 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-58874-4 Riemannian manifold dynamic attention fusion network for motor imagery EEG decoding
articles.content:
Abstract

A notable challenge encountered by motor imagery decoding algorithms utilizing electroencephalography (EEG) signals is the substantial redundancy and inadequate geometric representation of spatiotemporal features, which stem from the volume conduction effects inherent to the human head. This phenomenon can obscure essential information regarding motor intentions with noise or non-discriminative features. Although traditional decoding models have sought to alleviate redundancy through shallow attention mechanisms or feature selection in Euclidean space, they frequently neglect the intrinsic manifold geometric properties of EEG signals, such as the positive definiteness of covariance matrices. Additionally, static attention weights are often insufficient in dynamically capturing the cross-domain dependencies between spatiotemporal and spectral features. To address these limitations, we propose a novel spatiotemporal dynamic attention fusion network grounded in Riemannian manifolds (ST-MA-SENet) for EEG motor imagery decoding. ST-MA-SENet adeptly assesses the spatiotemporal correlations among EEG features in both Euclidean and Riemannian spaces from a comprehensive perspective, thereby facilitating the selection of a distinctive and effective EEG fusion feature for motor imagery recognition. To evaluate the efficacy of ST-MA-SENet, we conducted experiments utilizing three motor imagery datasets (BCI IV 2a, BCI IV 2b, HGD), and the results demonstrate that ST-MA-SENet represents a highly promising approach for EEG signal decoding.

articles.title: Data-driven and physics-inspired sound synthesis of ocean waves incorporating screened foam particles and projective space
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 03 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-59852-6 Data-driven and physics-inspired sound synthesis of ocean waves incorporating screened foam particles and projective space
articles.content:
Abstract

This paper proposes a data-driven approach to efficiently synthesize the spatial sound of ocean waves by integrating 2D projective space and screened foam particles. To reduce computational complexity, we present a framework that synthesizes ocean wave sounds by using screened foam particles to reduce the dimensionality of the computation domain. The technical contributions of this paper are as follows: (1) a method for stable clustering and continuously updating the labeling of screened foam particles in 2D projective space, (2) an anisotropic sound intensity attenuation technique that accounts for the distance and angular relationship between the audience and the sound source, (3) sound intensity control utilizing the relationships between density and temperature and between temperature and the speed of sound, (4) sound modulation based on the screened density of foam particles, and (5) a sound attenuation technique considering time-delay effects. The proposed method enables rapid sound synthesis, making it suitable for various real-time applications, such as games and metaverse environments, where computational efficiency and perceptually plausible audiovisual coherence are essential. It should be noted that the proposed framework does not aim to physically reproduce real-world three-dimensional ocean bubble acoustics, but rather to provide a foam-dynamics-driven approximation applicable to large-scale virtual ocean scenarios. Additionally, since the synthesis of ocean wave sounds relies solely on screened foam particles rather than full 3D fluid dynamics calculations, it requires relatively low memory and computational resources, making it computationally efficient for integration into real-time virtual ocean rendering and interactive simulation systems.

articles.title: SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket launches 24 Starlink satellites from California
articles.summary: A SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket carrying 24 Starlink satellites launched from Vandenberg Space Force Base in California on Wednesday, July 1, 2026.
articles.content:
SpaceX grew its Starlink network by 24 more satellites on Wednesday evening (July 1) with a launch from California.

A Falcon 9 rocket carrying the two dozen new relay spacecraft (Group 17-46) lifted off from Space Launch Complex 4 East at Vandenberg Space Force Base at 10:58 p.m. EDT (0258 GMT on July 2 or 7:58 p.m. PDT local time). About an hour later, SpaceX confirmed the satellites were successfully deployed in low Earth orbit.

The Falcon 9's first stage (Booster 1100) completed its seventh flight to space and back, landing on the drone ship "Of Course I Still Love You" positioned in the Pacific Ocean.

SpaceX's Starlink megaconstellation now totals more than 10,700 active satellites, according to tracker Jonathan McDowell. The service supports connectively around the globe, as well as in-flight and direct-to-cell services.

Wednesday's launch was SpaceX's 79th Falcon 9 liftoff of the year.

Pearlman is also a contributing writer for Space.com and co-author of "Space Stations: The Art, Science, and Reality of Working in Space” published by Smithsonian Books in 2018.
In 2009, he was inducted into the U.S. Space Camp Hall of Fame in Huntsville, Alabama. articles.title: SpaceX fires up all 6 of Starship's engines ahead of 13th test flight (video)
articles.summary: SpaceX completed a Starship engine test at its Starbase facility recently, igniting all six of the vehicle's engines for a full minute.
articles.content:
Less than a week after its last test, SpaceX's latest Starship spacecraft went back to the stand for engine checkouts ahead of a coming test flight.

The upper stage for SpaceX's giant Starship rocket, referred to simply as "Ship" — Ship 40, in this case — underwent a static-fire test of all six of its Raptor engines at the company's Massey site in Starbase, Texas. Ship 40 is in line for the 13th test flight of a fully stacked Starship, which is expected within the next month or so.

SpaceX posted a video of the engine test to its X account on Thursday (July 2), showing several angles of the ignition sequence and all six engines (Ship is equipped with three sea-level and three vacuum-optimized Raptors) firing to simulate flight-like conditions on the vehicle.

Starship 60-second static fire ahead of the thirteenth flight test pic.twitter.com/HKZkaXo7WNJuly 2, 2026

Ship 40 conducted its first-ever static fire last week, lighting a single Raptor for about 15 seconds. This more recent test involved all six Raptors and lasted a full minute, according to SpaceX.

Ship 40 will take part in the second "Version 3" (V3) Starship launch; SpaceX debuted the updated rocket prototype during a test flight on May 22. That mission went smoothly, for the most part, but it wasn't a complete success; the rocket's Super Heavy booster failed to maneuver its way to a soft ocean splashdown as planned. So the upcoming Flight 13 will likely mirror much of the flight path and mission objectives as Flight 12, including relighting one of Ship's Raptor engines in space.

Starship is SpaceX's super-heavy lift rocket, designed for seamless landing and reuse. The more capable V3 stands 408 feet (124.4 meters) tall — about 5 feet (1.5 m) taller than V2 — and is the biggest, most powerful rocket ever to fly.

Once development is complete and the vehicle is fully operational, SpaceX has big plans for the rocket, including the expansion of its Starlink satellite-internet network in low Earth orbit and landing NASA astronauts on the moon as a part of the Artemis program. The spacecraft will also be capable of delivering heavier payloads to orbit than any other rocket in history.

The success of this most recent engine test puts SpaceX one step closer to Starship Flight 13. Next steps ahead of that launch will include rolling the mission's Super Heavy booster to the pad at Starbase for engine tests of its own. Super Heavy sports 33 Raptor engines at its business end, which produce nearly 20 million pounds of thrust at liftoff. Should Super Heavy's static fire tests go smoothly, Flight 13 could happen as early as August.

articles.title: 'Silo' season 3: Release date & how to watch Apple TV's sensational post-apocalyptic saga
articles.summary: Say so long to the sunshine as we plunge back into the deep mysteries of Silo 18.
articles.content:
"Silo" season 3 is almost upon us, offering another round of riveting episodes of Apple TV's undeniably intriguing sci-fi series. We're about to learn how the end of the world began with the new season's split-timeline between Silo 18 and the Before Times, when the madness all started.

Adapted from Hugh Howey's trilogy of dystopian sci-fi novels, "Wool," "Shift," and "Dust," "Silo" was created by and executive produced by Emmy-winning producer Graham Yost ("Speed," "Band of Brothers," "Justified") and initially debuted on Apple TV back in 2023. This third season promises to be the best yet, and we guarantee that it's going to be an eye-opening descent into the core of the overarching tale of humanity’s last band of hearty heroes and nefarious villains trying to stay sane.

Dump out your memory-suppressing H2O and let's dig into "Silo" season 3!

WHEN DOES "SILO" SEASON 3 ARRIVE?

The third and penultimate season of "Silo" premieres on July 3, 2026, at 12:00 a.m. Eastern Time (ET) for the first episode of what’s going to be a wildly eventful 10-episode outing.

Fresh weekly episodes will arrive each Friday until the season finale is broadcast on Sept. 4, 2026.

HOW TO WATCH "SILO" SEASON 3

"Silo" season 3 will stream exclusively on Apple TV.

"Silo" is an Apple original that explores the last vestiges of humankind, surviving in 144-level doomsday bunkers, and the various factions of post-apocalyptic society struggling to exist and understand why the outside environment is a death sentence.

The first two seasons of "Silo" are also now available to watch in their entirety on Apple TV, so you can catch up on all the dark drama.

If you're going to be out of the country when the show debuts, you can still watch it on your streaming service of choice using a VPN.

You'll be able to connect to your streaming services, no matter where you are on Earth (though you might not have wifi if you're hiding in one of the Silos).

Travelling abroad and still want access to Apple TV? Good news, NordVPN can help you get around those pesky geoblocking restrictions so you can watch the streaming service you've paid for anywhere in the world.

WHAT IS THE PLOT OF "SILO" SEASON 3?

Warning: Spoilery "Silo" Season 2 Territory Ahead!

The prime timeline of "Silo" takes place in the far future, some 352 years from now, but nobody in Silo 18 is really counting, or even owns accurate calendars, now that we think about it.

Fans who've followed the show religiously know that Juliette trudged back from Silo 17 just before a mass Silo 18 exodus in last year’s finale episode after learning about a lethal safeguard installed in each bunker by the Founders to prevent rebellion.

She's met in the airlock by an unhinged and disillusioned Bernard (Tim Robbins) armed with a gun, and before you can say "Burnt Toast," the automatic fire system activates, and they’re both engulfed in raging flames.

That same episode ends with a flashback three centuries earlier to a restaurant in Washington, D.C., after a dirty bomb has been detonated in the city, where we're introduced to Helen and Daniel, two pivotal new characters that we’ll be following heavily in this upcoming outing.

"Season three of 'Silo' continues the saga of a dystopian society of 10,000 people living underground under mysterious circumstances, while revealing an origin story set centuries earlier," explains the official synopsis.

"In the present, Juliette Nichols (Rebecca Ferguson) survives her forced 'cleaning' but returns with memory loss as the silo recovers from rebellion and faces a dangerous new threat. Meanwhile, in the 'Before Times,' journalist Helen Drew (Jessica Henwick) and Congressman Daniel Keene (Ashley Zukerman) uncover a conspiracy that pulls them into a chain of events with catastrophic, irreversible consequences."

"SILO" SEASON 3 TEASERS AND TRAILERS

Sneak peeks at "Silo's" third season have been sparse, and with an abundance of startling reveals about to be discovered, fans should be grateful that the PR folks at Apple TV haven’t exposed too much as our survivors try to make sense of their depressing upside-down world.

The first reverse-edited teaser landed back on Apr. 21, 2026, which got us pumped for the pandemonium!

The main trailer debuted on June 2, 2026, and we were immediately brought up to speed about Juliette being inside the fire box for three minutes, but she's now experiencing amnesia and can’t remember her friends or what secret she uncovered while inside Silo 17.

We also gain more info on the silo Founders and the monumental 21st-century engineering project that created them to rescue humanity from extinction.

"The end of the world cannot be stopped, it can only be survived," states Colin Hanks' chilling billionaire industrialist character.

WHO ARE THE "SILO" SEASON 3 CAST AND CREATORS?

"Silo" season 3's outstanding ensemble cast includes:

  • Rebecca Ferguson – Juliette Nichols
  • Jessica Henwick – Helen
  • Ashley Zukerman – Daniel
  • Common – Robert Sims
  • Harriet Walter – Martha Walker
  • Chinaza Uche – Paul Billings
  • Avi Nash – Lukas Kyle
  • Alexandria Riley – Camille Sims
  • Shane McRae – Knox
  • Remmie Milner – Shirley Campbell
  • Rick Gomez – Patrick Kennedy
  • Billy Postlethwaite – Hank
  • Clare Perkins – Carla McLain
  • Steve Zahn – Jimmy Conroy

The following newcomers will also be joining the series in unknown roles:

  • Colin Hanks
  • Laura Innes
  • Jessica Brown Findlay
  • Morven Christie
  • Reed Birney
  • Matt Craven

Produced by Apple Studios and AMC Studios, "Silo"'s upcoming 10-chapter nightmare is executive produced by showrunner Graham Yost, Michael Dinner, Rebecca Ferguson, Nina Jack, Joanna Thapa, Morten Tyldum, Howey, Amber Templemore, Fred Golan, and Rémi Aubuchon.

WILL THERE BE A "SILO" SEASON 4?

Yes, we’re not out of the hole quite yet!

Apple TV gave a green light for both "Silo's" third and fourth seasons back in December of 2024 after overwhelming acclaim and impressive viewership numbers, guaranteeing a proper end to the "Silo" saga. Season 4 will be the final season, though.

"It has been a richly rewarding experience to adapt Hugh's epic novels with our partners at Apple, and we are thrilled to have the opportunity to bring this complete story to the screen over the course of four seasons," Yost shared after Apple TV’s renewal.

"With the final two chapters of 'Silo,' we can't wait to give fans of the show an incredibly satisfying conclusion to the many mysteries and unanswered questions contained within the walls of these silos."

articles.title: Apollo 11 landing site, a cosmic Eagle and a blue-white star: 4 night sky targets to celebrate America 250
articles.summary: From the Apollo landing site to the North America Nebula, these celestial sights offer a uniquely American way to celebrate the nation's 250th birthday.
articles.content:
The United States' 250th birthday is almost here, so why not take a break from the fireworks and explore four America-themed wonders hiding in the summer night sky?

Our celestial sightseeing targets run the gamut from lone stars and constellations to historic lunar landmarks and an uncannily shaped nebula — some of which will require a small telescope or a camera to truly appreciate.

If you're new to the night sky, then you may want to check out our roundup of the best stargazing smartphone apps, which will help you navigate to specific targets using augmented reality technology.

Apollo 11 landing site

One of America's finest moments came on July 20, 1969, as Apollo 11 astronauts Neil A. Armstrong and Edwin E. "Buzz" Aldrin Jr. took humanity's first steps on the surface of Earth's moon. Here's how to spot the region of the moon that contains the Apollo 11 "Tranquility Base" landing site with the naked eye on the weeks surrounding a full moon.

First, find the dark expanse of Mare Tranquilitatis darkening a swathe of the moon's eastern limb close to the equator, where ancient lava flows once filled networks of impact basins before solidifying to create vast basaltic plains.

The Apollo 11 landing site is located on the southwestern shore of the Sea of Tranquility, between the Sabine and Moltke impact craters, which can be spotted with the aid of a small telescope. You can also search for the Apollo 11 backup landing sites using our handy guide, or even attempt to find where each Apollo-era mission touched down on the lunar surface.

Spica

The United States' relationship with the night sky dates back to its founding. After all, the stars depicted on the nation's earliest flags were intended to symbolize the birth of a new constellation, with each representing one of the 13 original colonies that formed the early union, according to the Smithsonian Institution.

You can honor that spirit on the nation's semiquincentennial anniversary by finding a star whose light left its surface around the same time that the United States declared its independence!

The blue-white double star system Alpha Virginis — known better as Spica — is located roughly 250 light-years from Earth in the constellation Virgo. As such, the light cast out from its surface some 250 years ago is only now reaching Earth, having traveled for centuries through the near vacuum of space at the speed of light — a blistering 186,282 miles per second (299,791 kilometers per second).

To find Spica, you first need to locate the seven bright stars of the Big Dipper asterism above the northwestern horizon after sunset, and trace an imaginary line along the curve of the Big Dipper's handle. Do that, and you'll come across the bright orange star Arcturus, the 4th brightest star in the night sky. Continue that arc beyond Arcturus and the next bright blue-white star you find will be Spica!

The constellation Aquila

Next up, we have the constellation Aquila, the "Eagle", which shines along the glowing ribbon of the Milky Way in early summer. The constellation evokes the shape of a great Bald Eagle, which was adopted as a national emblem of the United States in 1782. Aquila itself has been associated with an eagle for thousands of years and was catalogued by the ancient Greek astronomer Claudius Ptolemy in the second century AD.

A good way to find Aquila is to search for the bright star Altair, which can be seen glowing halfway up the southern sky to the left of the Milky Way for viewers in the U.S. around midnight in early summer. Altair, along with the nearby stars Alshain and Tarazed, represent the head of the great eagle, while the body and raised wings of the celestial bird are depicted in a cross formation of stars beneath.

North America Nebula

Our final stop is the North American Nebula — a gorgeous emission nebula whose vast glowing clouds of interstellar hydrogen resemble the outline of North America.

The North America Nebula is too faint to see with the naked eye. However, a pair of 10x50 binoculars or a small telescope should reveal the star-forming region as a gentle brightening in the band of the Milky Way from a dark sky location, less than five degrees from the bright star Deneb, which shines above the eastern horizon after sunset.

Its structure is best seen through long exposure photography, which will help reveal the dusty filaments and chaotic cosmic clouds that form the nebula. Read our beginner's guide to photographing the Milky Way for more!

Want to get a closer look at the night sky? Then be sure to check out our roundups of the best telescopes and binoculars for stargazing, along with our expert tips for beginning your amateur astronomy journey.
